abstract:
Characterization of the non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) present in biomass burning (BB) emissions is essential for modeling secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation and its subsequent impacts on regional air quality and global climate. In this study, gaseous organic emissions from the laboratory combustion of six globally significant vegetative fuels—including conifer branches, agricultural residues, peat, and grasses—were collected on sorbent cartridges during the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4). Samples were analyzed using two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). Taking advantage of the high sensitivity and peak capacity of this technique, the authors provided the most comprehensive speciation of BB emissions to date, identifying or tentatively identifying 708 organic compounds. 

The results highlight significant chemical diversity across fuels and reveal extensive isomer groups within several compound classes. Notably, the study reports 38 monoterpene isomers in emissions from coniferous fuels; the distribution of these isomers closely matches the profiles of plant-specific essential oils, suggesting a potential method for estimating terpene emissions. Additionally, 11 sesquiterpene isomers were speciated, offering the first such detailed data for these high-yield SOA precursors in BB smoke. Emission factors were calculated for all identified species and examined in the context of their potential to generate SOA. By quantifying a broad range of intermediate-volatility and reactive compounds, this research addresses key uncertainties in current emission inventories and provides a robust dataset for improving photochemical models of biomass burning plumes.
